questions.question: Which of the following is a relational operator in Java?
What is the result of the expression: 10 != 10?
What is the key difference between == and = in Java?
options: == is for assignment and = is for comparison
== is for comparison and = is for assignment
== works only with strings and = works with all types
questions.answer: == is for comparison and = is for assignment

questions.question: Why can char values be compared using relational operators like < and >?
options: Because char is a subclass of String
Because char values have underlying numeric Unicode values
Because Java automatically converts chars to Strings before comparison
Because all Java types support relational operators
questions.answer: Because char values have underlying numeric Unicode values

questions.question: What is the result of: 'A' == 65?
What does the && operator return?
options: true if either operand is true
true only if both operands are true
true if both operands are false
The value of the left operand
questions.answer: true only if both operands are true

questions.question: What is the result of: true || false?
What does the ! (logical NOT) operator do?
options: Flips true to false and false to true
Checks if a value is not null
questions.answer: Flips true to false and false to true

questions.question: What is short-circuit evaluation?
options: Java evaluates both sides of a logical operator regardless of the result
Java stops evaluating a logical expression as soon as the result is determined
Java skips the entire expression if a variable is null
Java converts boolean expressions to integers before evaluating
questions.answer: Java stops evaluating a logical expression as soon as the result is determined

questions.question: In the expression (x == 0) || (10 / x > 2) where x is 0, why does no exception occur?
options: Because Java catches all ArithmeticExceptions automatically
Because the left side is true, so the right side is never evaluated due to short-circuit evaluation
Because dividing by zero returns 0 in Java
Because || always evaluates both sides but ignores exceptions
questions.answer: Because the left side is true, so the right side is never evaluated due to short-circuit evaluation

questions.question: What is the correct syntax of the ternary operator in Java?
options: if condition then valueIfTrue else valueIfFalse

questions.question: Why are nested ternary operators generally discouraged?
options: They are slower than if/else chains
They reduce readability and are hard to maintain
They are not supported in Java
questions.answer: They reduce readability and are hard to maintain

questions.question: What is the result of: 2 + 3 * 4?
What is the result of: (2 + 3) * 4?
In Java operator precedence, which operator has higher precedence: && or ||?
options: Precedence depends on the data types used

questions.question: What is the result of: true || false && false?
In Java's operator precedence, which of the following has the HIGHEST precedence?
What is the result of: boolean c = 10 > 5 + 3;?
options: false, because 5 + 3 is evaluated first giving 8, and 10 > 8 is true
true, because 5 + 3 is evaluated first giving 8, and 10 > 8 is true
questions.answer: true, because 5 + 3 is evaluated first giving 8, and 10 > 8 is true
